Title: Medicinal Tropane Alkaloids: Biosynthesis, Evolution, and Engineering

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pharmaceutical anticholinergic drugs for treating various disorders
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Used as a selectable marker gene together with H6H in transgenic A. belladonna; associated with increased scopolamine and anisodamine and decreased hyoscyamine in field-tolerant lines, but not a direct TA biosynthetic gene
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. G2-EPSPS from Pseudomonas fluorescens was used as a marker gene in A. belladonna transformation. 2. Combined with H6H, it enabled transgenic homozygous lines with high scopolamine production. 3. It conferred strong herbicide tolerance in the field.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic plant generation; field evaluation; metabolite profiling
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This is not a TA pathway or regulatory gene; it functions as a transformation/selection marker linked to herbicide tolerance.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful as a marker gene for generating herbicide-tolerant transgenic TA-producing A. belladonna lines when stacked with H6H.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Atropa belladonna and other Solanaceae TA-producing species
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Decarboxylates ornithine to produce putrescine in the shared initial pathway of nicotine and TA biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pharmaceutical anticholinergic drugs for treating various disorders
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes TA precursor formation and can increase putrescine, N-methylputrescine, hyoscyamine and anisodamine in transgenic A. belladonna; scopolamine unchanged in the cited engineering case.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ODC is an upstream enzyme in Module I converting ornithine to putrescine. 2. It participates in the shared entry pathway for nicotine and tropane alkaloid biosynthesis. 3. Overexpression in A. belladonna increased putrescine, N-methylputrescine, hyoscyamine and anisodamine, but not scopolamine.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Pathway elucidation; transgenic overexpression in plants/hairy roots; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Single-gene overexpression has limited ability to boost final TA yield.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Used in engineering individually and in combination with H6H.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for precursor boosting, especially in gene stacks with downstream enzymes such as H6H.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Atropa belladonna and other Solanaceae TA-producing species
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers a methyl group to putrescine to produce N-methylputrescine in the shared initial pathway of nicotine and TA biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pharmaceutical anticholinergic drugs for treating various disorders
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports flux into TA biosynthesis; co-overexpression with H6H in A. belladonna decreases hyoscyamine and increases anisodamine/scopolamine.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PMT catalyzes methylation of putrescine early in TA biosynthesis. 2. It is shared with nicotine biosynthesis. 3. Co-expression with H6H synergistically enhanced downstream medicinal TAs in transgenic A. belladonna.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Pathway elucidation; transgenic overexpression; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Can shift metabolite balance away from hyoscyamine when stacked with downstream enzymes.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Widely used in hairy root engineering and whole-plant engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Good candidate for stacking with H6H or other downstream genes to improve scopolamine production.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Atropa belladonna and other Solanaceae TA-producing species
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pharmaceutical anticholinergic drugs for treating various disorders
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Produces a key intermediate required for tropinone formation and downstream TA biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MPO is part of the shared initial pathway with nicotine biosynthesis. 2. It converts N-methylputrescine into N-methyl-Δ1-pyrrolinium. 3. This intermediate feeds into tropinone and medicinal TA formation.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mentioned as one of the elucidated TA biosynthetic enzymes, but not specifically highlighted for engineering in this review passage.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential precursor-supply engineering target in multi-gene pathway reconstruction.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Atropa belladonna and other Solanaceae TA-producing species
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Solanaceae TA-producing plants; hairy root cultures
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Acts with tropinone synthase (CYP82M3) to produce tropinone from N-methyl-Δ1-pyrrolinium.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pharmaceutical anticholinergic drugs for treating various disorders
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for tropinone formation and contributes to engineering of TA biosynthesis in hairy roots.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PYKS is a Module I enzyme in TA biosynthesis. 2. Together with CYP82M3, it forms tropinone from N-methyl-Δ1-pyrrolinium. 3. It has been tested in hairy root engineering for TA production.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review states PYKS and CYP82M3 jointly generate tropinone.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful in pathway reconstruction or multi-gene stacks for TA production.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Atropa belladonna and other Solanaceae species
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Functions with PYKS to produce tropinone from N-methyl-Δ1-pyrrolinium.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pharmaceutical anticholinergic drugs for treating various disorders
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for tropinone production and early TA pathway flux.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP82M3 is the tropinone synthase in Module I. 2. A CYP82M3-TRI gene pair is present in both TA-producing and nonproducing Solanaceae species. 3. It is one of the genes investigated in hairy root engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative genomics; pathway elucidation; hairy root engineering
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Conservation of CYP82M3 with TRI supports ancestral presence of early TA pathway in Solanaceae.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for pathway reconstruction in nonproducing Solanaceae when combined with missing downstream genes.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Atropa belladonna and other Solanaceae species
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Solanaceae species including Solanum tuberosum; hairy root cultures
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces tropinone to tropine, the direct precursor for medicinal TAs.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pharmaceutical anticholinergic drugs for treating various disorders
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enables tropine formation; loss or inactivation blocks TA production potential, while restored activity could re-enable pathway flux.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TRI converts tropinone to tropine. 2. The TRI gene is inactivated by a point mutation in Solanum tuberosum. 3. Its activity can be restored by targeted nucleotide substitution, supporting evolutionary loss rather than absence of the early pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative genomics; targeted nucleotide substitution; pathway elucidation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: A key evolutionary example in the review; present in both producing and nonproducing species but can be nonfunctional in the latter.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A candidate for precise editing to restore early TA biosynthesis in nonproducing Solanaceae such as potato.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts phenylalanine into phenylpyruvate in the acyl-donor branch of TA biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pharmaceutical anticholinergic drugs for treating various disorders
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Provides phenylpyruvate precursor for phenyllactate/phenyllactylglucose synthesis required for littorine formation.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ArAT4 initiates Module II from phenylalanine. 2. It converts phenylalanine to phenylpyruvate. 3. This branch supplies the acyl donor used later for littorine synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Module II links phenylalanine metabolism to TA biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for increasing acyl-donor supply in full-pathway engineering.

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pharmaceutical anticholinergic drugs for treating various disorders
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Generates phenyllactate needed for glycosylation and littorine synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PPAR acts downstream of ArAT4 in Module II. 2. It reduces phenylpyruvate to phenyllactate. 3. Phenyllactate is the substrate for UGT1 to generate the acyl donor for littorine synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential module-II engineering target for precursor-channeling to littorine.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Atropa belladonna; hairy root cultures from TA-producing species
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes phenyllactate glycosylation using UDP-glucose to produce phenyllactylglucose, the acyl donor for littorine synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pharmaceutical anticholinergic drugs for treating various disorders
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Produces the acyl donor directly required for littorine synthesis and is used in hairy root engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. UGT1 converts phenyllactate and UDP-glucose into phenyllactylglucose. 2. Phenyllactylglucose is the acyl donor for littorine synthesis. 3. UGT1 has been investigated in hairy root engineering of TA production.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Enzyme function elucidation; hairy root engineering
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate for inclusion in complete pathway reconstruction and multi-gene engineering.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Atropa belladonna; nonproducing Solanaceae; hairy root cultures
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses phenyllactylglucose and tropine through esterification to form littorine.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pharmaceutical anticholinergic drugs for treating various disorders
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enables littorine formation, a required precursor for downstream hyoscyamine/scopolamine biosynthesis; loss contributes to TA deficiency in nonproducing Solanaceae.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LS is the sole enzyme in Module III and forms littorine by esterifying phenyllactylglucose with tropine. 2. LS belongs to the serine carboxypeptidase-like acyltransferase family. 3. LS is missing or disrupted in nonproducing Solanaceae species, and reintroduction is suggested to restore TA biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Enzyme identification; comparative genomics; hairy root engineering
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: A lineage-specific loss highlighted as an explanation for uneven TA distribution in Solanaceae.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High-value target for reconstructing TA biosynthesis in nonproducing Solanaceae and in synthetic systems.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Atropa belladonna; nonproducing Solanaceae; hairy root cultures
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes oxidation and rearrangement of littorine to generate hyoscyamine aldehyde.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pharmaceutical anticholinergic drugs for treating various disorders
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for conversion of littorine toward hyoscyamine and downstream scopolamine; missing or disrupted in nonproducing Solanaceae.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP80F1 is the littorine mutase of Module IV. 2. It converts littorine to hyoscyamine aldehyde through oxidation and rearrangement. 3. CYP80F1 is missing or disrupted in several nonproducing Solanaceae species, making it a key restoration target.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Enzyme identification; comparative genomics; hairy root engineering
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Loss of CYP80F1 is specifically linked to inability to produce hyoscyamine/scopolamine in nonproducing species.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Priority engineering target for pathway completion in heterologous plants or microbes.

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pharmaceutical anticholinergic drugs for treating various disorders
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Produces hyoscyamine, the direct precursor of anisodamine and scopolamine.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HDH/HAR is an oxidoreductase in Module IV. 2. It converts hyoscyamine aldehyde to hyoscyamine. 3. It has been investigated in hairy root engineering of TA production.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review notes alternative naming as HAR or HDH.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important downstream engineering target for securing hyoscyamine supply before H6H-mediated conversion.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Atropa belladonna; Hyoscyamus niger and other TA-producing plants
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Atropa belladonna; Anisodus acutangulus hairy roots; hairy root cultures from TA-producing species
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Activated in A. acutangulus hairy roots by AaWRKY11
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes 6β-hydroxylation of hyoscyamine to form anisodamine and then epoxidation of anisodamine to form scopolamine.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key terminal determinant supported by engineering emphasis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pharmaceutical anticholinergic drugs for treating various disorders
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes conversion of hyoscyamine into anisodamine and scopolamine; overexpression decreases hyoscyamine and increases anisodamine/scopolamine in A. belladonna.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. H6H catalyzes the final hydroxylation and epoxidation steps from hyoscyamine to scopolamine via anisodamine. 2. Overexpression in A. belladonna decreased hyoscyamine and increased anisodamine/scopolamine. 3. H6H from Hyoscyamus niger shows superior performance for engineering scopolamine production. 4. Stacking H6H with ODC or PMT further enhances downstream TA accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Enzyme identification; transgenic overexpression in plants; hairy root engineering; metabolite profiling; TF activation evidence
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced hyoscyamine pool due to enhanced conversion to downstream products.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Highlighted as an especially valuable engineering gene and one of the best-performing enzymes for scopolamine production.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Top-priority engineering target for increasing scopolamine, especially using high-activity orthologs such as H. niger H6H and stacking with upstream pathway genes.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Low nitrogen indirectly relevant via LNIR activation

Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-specific positive regulator that increases scopolamine production by activating the terminal pathway gene H6H.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pharmaceutical anticholinergic drug for treating various disorders
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes scopolamine biosynthesis in A. acutangulus hairy roots.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AaWRKY11 is a positive transcriptional regulator in A. acutangulus. 2. It activates H6H expression. 3. This activation promotes scopolamine biosynthesis in hairy roots.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Hairy root functional analysis; target-gene activation evidence
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review presents AaWRKY11 as a promising engineering regulator for TA production.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Can be combined with terminal biosynthetic genes such as H6H to coordinately elevate scopolamine production.

Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Nutrient-responsive positive regulator coordinating multiple pathway genes and increasing overall TA accumulation.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pharmaceutical anticholinergic drugs for treating various disorders
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Increases TA accumulation in A. belladonna hairy root cultures under/with low-nitrogen response by activating multiple biosynthetic genes.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LNIR is a low-nitrogen-responsive bHLH transcription factor in A. belladonna. 2. It activates multiple TA biosynthetic genes. 3. LNIR increases TA accumulation in hairy root cultures.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Hairy root functional analysis; transcriptional activation evidence; metabolite profiling
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review suggests combining transcription factors like LNIR with rate-limiting enzymes to enhance TA production.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High application value as a multiplex regulator, especially for stacking with key enzymes such as H6H in native plants or synthetic systems.
